name: E2B
competence: Secure cloud sandboxes for AI agent code execution
targetUser: AI agent developers who need secure, isolated environments for running AI-generated code in production
description: E2B is an open-source infrastructure platform that provides secure, isolated cloud sandboxes specifically built for AI agent workflows. Powered by Firecracker microVMs, sandboxes start in under 200ms and support any programming language that runs on Linux. E2B is not a general compute platform — it's purpose-built for agent developers who need to run AI-generated code safely. 88% of Fortune 100 companies are signed up, and the platform is used by organizations like Hugging Face (for RL pipelines) and Groq (for compound AI systems). Starting December 2025, every sandbox includes access to Docker's MCP Catalog with 200+ tools.
oneLiner: Open-source secure cloud sandboxes for AI agent code execution, starting in under 200ms.
funding: Series A: $21M led by Insight Partners. Participants: Decibel, Sunflower Capital, Kaya. Angels include Docker's former CEO Scott Johnston.
pricing: Hobby: Free ($100 credit, 1hr sessions, 20 concurrent sandboxes). Pro: $150/month (24hr sessions, more concurrency, custom CPU/RAM). Enterprise: Custom (BYOC, on-prem, self-hosted). Usage: ~$0.05/hour per 1 vCPU, billed per second.
capabilities: Any language that runs on Linux
E2B Desktop (GUI sandbox for computer use)

uxAnalysis.builderFlow: Developer-first experience: install the SDK (pip or npm), create a sandbox with one line of code, execute commands or code within it. No visual builder — this is infrastructure, not an agent builder. The SDK is clean and well-documented with clear examples for Python and JS/TS.
strengths: Fastest sandbox startup in the market (sub-200ms)
Clean, minimal SDK with excellent documentation
Language-agnostic — runs anything that runs on Linux
Docker MCP Catalog integration gives agents 200+ tools out of the box
weaknesses: Not an agent builder — purely infrastructure/execution layer
No built-in agent orchestration or multi-agent coordination
No governance, guardrails, or policy enforcement layer
Requires integration with a separate agent framework (LangGraph, CrewAI, etc.)
notableFeatures: E2B Desktop: GUI sandbox for computer-use agents
Custom sandbox templates for reproducible environments
Docker MCP Gateway integration for 200+ tool access

subCriteria.name: Builder UX
subCriteria.rationale: No visual builder — SDK-only, code-first. Excellent for developers but no low-code option for business users.

subCriteria.name: Time to First Agent
subCriteria.rationale: E2B is infrastructure, not an agent framework. You need a separate framework (LangChain, CrewAI) to build agents — E2B provides the execution sandbox.

subCriteria.name: Debugging & Testing
subCriteria.rationale: Terminal access and file inspection within sandboxes. Error output is captured. But no agent-level debugging — just code execution debugging.

subCriteria.name: Documentation Quality
subCriteria.rationale: Excellent documentation with clear quickstarts, SDK references, and examples. Well-organized for the infrastructure scope it covers.

subCriteria.name: Multi-Agent Coordination
subCriteria.rationale: Not an orchestration platform. Zero built-in multi-agent support. Must be paired with an orchestration framework.

subCriteria.name: Human-in-the-Loop
subCriteria.rationale: No HITL mechanisms. Sandboxes execute code autonomously — any HITL must be built in the calling framework.

subCriteria.name: Workflow Templates
subCriteria.rationale: Custom sandbox templates allow reproducible environments, but these are infrastructure templates, not agent workflow templates.

subCriteria.name: Team Development
subCriteria.rationale: Pro and Enterprise tiers support team usage. API keys and usage tracking available. But no collaborative agent development features.

subCriteria.name: Native Connectors
subCriteria.rationale: Docker MCP Catalog integration provides 200+ tools (GitHub, Perplexity, Browserbase, ElevenLabs). This is E2B's strongest integration story.

subCriteria.name: API & Standards
subCriteria.rationale: Clean REST API plus Python and JS/TS SDKs. Works with all major LLM providers and agent frameworks. Well-designed API surface.

subCriteria.name: Auth & Identity
subCriteria.rationale: API key authentication. Enterprise tier supports BYOC and on-prem. Service account management available.

subCriteria.name: Data Pipeline / RAG
subCriteria.rationale: Sandboxes can access files, install packages, and run data processing code. Not a RAG platform but can execute RAG pipelines within sandboxes.

subCriteria.name: Guardrails & Safety
subCriteria.rationale: Security through isolation — Firecracker microVMs prevent sandbox escape. But no content guardrails, output filtering, or policy enforcement on what agents DO within the sandbox.

subCriteria.name: Audit & Logging
subCriteria.rationale: Usage tracking and billing logs. Sandbox lifecycle events logged. But no agent decision audit trails or chain-of-thought logging.

subCriteria.name: RBAC & Permissions
subCriteria.rationale: Team management on Enterprise tier. API key scoping. But no fine-grained RBAC for agent permissions within sandboxes.

subCriteria.name: Explainability
subCriteria.rationale: Sandboxes capture stdout/stderr and execution results. But no explainability for agent reasoning — that's the calling framework's job.

subCriteria.name: Deployment Flexibility
subCriteria.rationale: Cloud (managed), BYOC, on-prem, and self-hosted options on Enterprise. Good flexibility for infrastructure deployment.

subCriteria.name: Scaling
subCriteria.rationale: Concurrent sandbox limits scale with tier. Sub-200ms startup enables burst scaling. Per-second billing is cost-efficient.

subCriteria.name: Cost Management
subCriteria.rationale: Per-second billing with clear per-vCPU pricing. Usage dashboards available. But no cost optimization recommendations or autoscaling policies.

subCriteria.name: Ecosystem & Marketplace
subCriteria.rationale: Open-source with Apache 2.0 license. Docker partnership. Growing ecosystem but no agent marketplace or template gallery.

scoringRationale: 48/100 overall. Strongest: Integration (14/20). Weakest: Orchestration (6/20). Breakdown: Creation (8/20), Orchestration (6/20), Integration (14/20), Governance (10/20), Operations (10/20).

walkthrough.title: How E2B Works: Secure Sandboxes for AI Agent Execution

steps.title: Docker MCP Integration
steps.description: E2B sandboxes integrated with Docker MCP catalog. Any MCP-compatible agent can use E2B for secure code execution.

works: Docker MCP integration means framework-agnostic sandbox access
Any agent that supports MCP can use E2B — no custom integration needed
Docker partnership adds credibility and distribution
doesntWork: MCP is still early — not all frameworks support it yet
Docker dependency adds another infrastructure layer
pmCritique.userPsychology: Docker MCP integration is E2B's distribution play — instead of integrating with every framework individually, they integrate with MCP and get all frameworks at once.

ctoCritique.verdict: MCP integration is the right strategic bet. As MCP adoption grows, E2B becomes the default sandbox. But MCP maturity is a risk factor.
enterpriseGaps: No MCP certification or compatibility testing

steps.title: Sandbox Configuration
steps.description: Template and runtime configuration for isolated agent environments. Resource allocation and lifecycle management.

pmCritique.userPsychology: Sandbox configuration is E2B's core competency — making isolation simple and reproducible.

ctoCritique.verdict: Template-based sandboxes mean we can standardize environments across teams. But template management needs governance.

steps.title: Agent Executing in Sandbox
steps.description: Agent running commands and modifying files inside an isolated E2B sandbox. Full filesystem and network access within the sandbox boundary.

works: Full agent capability within isolation boundary — best of both worlds
Real terminal output shows actual agent work
Sandbox boundary prevents any host system impact
doesntWork: Agent activity only visible in terminal logs
No web UI for sandbox inspection
pmCritique.userPsychology: The sandbox execution is E2B's 'it just works' moment — agents have full power but can't break anything.

ctoCritique.verdict: This is the trust layer. Without sandbox isolation, we can't deploy agents that modify code or run commands in production.
concerns: Sandbox networking and egress rules unclear
enterpriseGaps: No sandbox forensics for post-incident analysis

pmVerdict.summary: E2B is the infrastructure layer for AI agent execution — secure sandboxes where agents run code without breaking anything. Not an agent builder, but the essential plumbing every agent platform needs. Docker MCP catalog integration means any MCP-compatible agent can use E2B sandboxes. 88% Fortune 100 adoption validates the enterprise value. Best for: teams building agent platforms who need secure code execution.
strengths: Only purpose-built sandbox infrastructure for AI agents
Docker MCP integration — any MCP agent can use E2B
Open-source + cloud — both deployment models available
risks: Not an agent builder — infrastructure only
Sandbox startup latency may impact real-time agents
Usage-based pricing at scale requires budget modeling
Invisible to end users — hard to justify as a standalone purchase

ctoVerdict.summary: E2B solves the #1 security concern with AI agents: 'what if the agent breaks something?' Managed sandboxes with full isolation, resource monitoring, and automatic cleanup. The Docker MCP catalog integration means any agent framework can use E2B. Buy decision: if you're building agents that execute code, E2B is a required infrastructure component.
ctoVerdict.decision: BUY — Required Infrastructure for Code-Executing Agents
strengths: Full isolation — agents can't break the host or each other
Docker MCP integration for framework-agnostic usage
$21M Series A from Insight Partners — financially stable
dealBreakers: Infrastructure-only — still need an agent framework on top
Sandbox cold start latency for real-time applications
Usage-based pricing requires careful budget modeling
Limited visibility into what agents do inside sandboxes

scoringAlignment.videoEvidence: E2B doesn't create agents — it provides execution environments
scoringAlignment.gap: Creation score reflects SDK quality, not agent building

scoringAlignment.videoEvidence: Fork-based parallel execution demonstrated in earlier steps
scoringAlignment.gap: Orchestration is at the sandbox level, not agent level

scoringAlignment.videoEvidence: Docker MCP integration, SDK demos, webhook support all shown
scoringAlignment.gap: Integration breadth is the core value proposition

scoringAlignment.videoEvidence: Isolation and resource monitoring provide basic governance
scoringAlignment.gap: No compliance certifications or audit trails shown

scoringAlignment.videoEvidence: Dashboard monitoring, budget controls, resource metrics shown

youtubeReviews.title: E2B Agent Sandboxes: The Space to Place your Claude Agents
Running AI Agents in Secure Sandboxes with E2B & Docker MCP

communityBuzz: E2B is the de facto standard for AI agent sandboxing — used by 88% of Fortune 100 (https://e2b.dev/blog/series-a)
Docker partnership (Dec 2025) adds 200+ MCP tools to every sandbox, significantly expanding what agents can do in isolation
Hugging Face uses E2B for reinforcement learning pipelines; Groq uses it for compound AI systems
AI sandbox benchmark (2026) shows E2B with the most mature SDK and largest community vs Modal and Daytona (https://www.superagent.sh/blog/ai-code-sandbox-benchmark-2026)
Apache 2.0 license makes it the most permissive option for enterprise adoption

recentDevelopments: Series A: Raised $21M led by Insight Partners to scale the enterprise AI agent cloud
Docker MCP Integration (Dec 2025): Every sandbox now includes Docker's MCP Catalog with 200+ tools
E2B Desktop: Launched GUI sandbox for computer-use agents (visual interaction with desktop environments)
Fortune 100 milestone: 88% of Fortune 100 companies signed up on the platform
AI sandbox benchmark leader: Ranked #1 for SDK maturity and community size vs Modal and Daytona

deploymentOptions.notes: Managed cloud, BYOC, on-prem, and self-hosted options on Enterprise tier.

coreCompetence.bestAt: The fastest, most secure sandboxed code execution for AI agents — sub-200ms Firecracker microVM startup, purpose-built for agent workflows, not general compute.
coreCompetence.targetUser: AI agent developers who need secure, isolated environments for running AI-generated code in production. Platform teams building agent infrastructure.
coreCompetence.vsAlternatives: Wins against Modal and Daytona on startup speed and SDK maturity for agent-specific use cases. Wins on security model (Firecracker microVMs). Loses — it is infrastructure, not an agent builder. Must be paired with an orchestration framework.
